segments.text: Laura, I really appreciate you joining
us here today. Um, and excited to get
into the conversation.
>> Yeah, likewise. Thanks for having me.
>> Well, we definitely appreciate you
taking the time and all of the effort to
come over here to do this. Um, I've
really enjoyed getting to know you and
all the work that you are doing with
engineering leaders, engineers, all the
data that you look at.
Um, I would love to know based on what
you're hearing these days, um, what does
organizational readiness look like for
engineering teams as they speed towards
>> Yeah, this is a question on every
engineering leader's mind right now. In
the past, when we brought a new
technology into our organization,
especially when it was something as
technical as AI technology, there was a
really uh there was a lot of emphasis on
the technology itself. So if we had the
right technology, we believed we would
get the right results. And with AI, that
is just proven not to be the case. When
we think about AI readiness um and
organizational readiness for AI, in
fact, the tooling choices are some of
the least impactful choices um and some
of the least impactful factors toward
overall organizational success. So tool
choice, of course, we need a tool that's
capable of of fulfilling the use case,
but other things like having a clear and
communicated stance about what AI is for
from your executive team is incredibly
important to keep everyone aligned and
working toward the same goals. Things
like uh great structured data models,
great developer experience, um time for
training, structured enablement, those
kinds of factors are bringing
organizations faster toward their goals
than just the the technology itself. And
in fact, organizations that don't invest
in things like great testing, culture,
quality practices, you know, clear and
communicated AI stance, user- centric
focus, focus on problems are seeing the
opposite of progress with AI. It's
actually kind of propelling them toward
um, you know, long lasting damage or
giving them some outcomes that they're
not very excited about. So, it is really
a big problem and something on the minds
of of every technology leader right now.
>> That's interesting. Do you find um are
companies doing well with with kind of
measuring the outcomes they're seeing?
Are they are they able to to
qualify that they're not seeing what
they want to see and take different
steps? It's really difficult to measure
the impact of AI right now, especially
tying it to like a P&L or a revenue.
There's still so many hop skips and
jumps that go from engineering
innovation all the way to revenue. and
we're just not quite there yet in terms
of maturity in terms of measuring
outcomes. One thing that I'm seeing
organizations get really tripped up in
is that they don't actually have an
outcome in mind when they bring AI into
their organization. So they hear the
hype, they they feel the pressure and
you know I would make the same decision
myself. They bring in AI and say well
this is a technology that is promising
all these productivity gains. It's
promising all this like you know really
futuristic advancement of engineering. I
got to have it. They're getting pressure
from their boards, from their their

copilot or codeex or cloud code or

the engineers are going to figure out
exactly what the best use cases are."
And that has proven time and time again
at the organizations I've studied just
not to be the case unfortunately because
the system around all of those coding
tasks that are now being sped up is
still a constraint. It's still broken
and we need a lot more focus on endtoend
value stream outcomes and not just

Are there any kind of key

a short a short list of of things to

you know we're in San Francisco right

because

fullText: Laura, I really appreciate you joining us here today. Um, and excited to get into the conversation. >> Yeah, likewise. When we think about AI readiness um and organizational readiness for AI, in fact, the tooling choices are some of the least impactful choices um and some of the least impactful factors toward overall organizational success. Things like uh great structured data models, great developer experience, um time for training, structured enablement, those kinds of factors are bringing organizations faster toward their goals than just the the technology itself. One thing that I'm seeing organizations get really tripped up in is that they don't actually have an outcome in mind when they bring AI into their organization. Like before agentic workflows when it was autocomplete, cognitive load was going down because it's like, oh, I just don't have to remember as much.
